title: Notifying Credit Bureaus to Prevent Identity Theft
summary: Deceased individuals are a common target for identity theft — notifying credit bureaus promptly closes that door.
category: After a Loss II: Notifications & Accounts
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Notifying Credit Bureaus to Prevent Identity Theft | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Deceased individuals are a common target for identity theft — notifying credit bureaus promptly closes that door.
whatYouNeedToKnow: The three major credit bureaus should be notified individually.
A "deceased alert" prevents new accounts from being opened in the person's name.
This step is often overlooked but genuinely worth doing early.

paragraphs: Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion each maintain separate records, so notifying one does not automatically notify the others — each needs its own notification, typically requiring a copy of the death certificate.
Once notified, bureaus place a "deceased" flag on the credit file, which helps prevent scammers from opening new credit in the person's name — a real and surprisingly common form of exploitation.

nextSteps: Contact all three major credit bureaus individually.
Have several death certificate copies ready for this step.
Request a copy of the final credit report to check for existing suspicious activity.
disclaimer: General guidance — each bureau's specific submission process may differ slightly.
